chapters.title: Other people can influence your output
chapters.text: Section placeholder. This opening entry collects author and contributor names tied to related Headway titles. The names below appear in the original source for reference. Daniel Lieberman, MD, Michael E. Long. Lisa Damour, PhD. David Richo, Ph.D. Gabrielle Bernstein.

chapters.title: The source of true wealth and fulfillment
chapters.text:
Here is what the billionaire taught our two companions about real success. Every person who has left a lasting mark on history shares one trait. They stopped chasing personal gain and started serving something bigger than themselves.

You can find true wealth and fulfillment by helping and inspiring others. He also pushed back against today's social economy, which runs on the idea that people need to "grow up" to become successful. He believes we often forget to appreciate small things and, in contrast, grow used to the ordinary.

Appearances can be deceiving. It is what lies underneath that truly matters.

The artist and the entrepreneur, drawn to the life and ideas of their new acquaintance, later met up with the billionaire to begin a mentorship that opened their eyes.

First and foremost, their new teacher emphasized the importance of waking at 5 am and kicking off tasks that would fuel the rest of the day. He told them he had discovered his own ability to use this strategy and that it had helped him live an above average life.

Starting your day in morning solitude helps you hold onto the focus you need to make the most of your daily tasks.

According to the billionaire, generating high quality output is one of the benefits of joining the 5 am club. Drawing on his rise from mediocrity to the exceptional, he told his guests how he had learned to block every source of distraction.

He backed this up with findings from neuroscientists that our peak mental state sharpens our reasoning, which drives our perception to a new level. Combining that powerful state with a proven method, which the billionaire called the 20/20/20 formula, improves prosperity, productivity, and performance.

The formula divides the first "Victory Hour" of the day, from 5:00 am to 6:00 am, into three twenty minute blocks. These blocks are dedicated to "Move" (intense exercise), "Reflect" (journaling, meditation, planning), and "Grow" (reading, learning, reviewing goals). They aim to optimize mindset, emotions, body, and spirit for higher productivity and well being. This concept helped the billionaire sharpen his thinking and become a visionary.

That line about rising before dawn comes from Aristotle.

chapters.title: The most successful people dedicate their time to the tiniest of details
chapters.text: Section placeholder. This entry lists contributor names from related Headway summaries. The names below appear in the original source for reference. Dr. Jerold J. Kreisman, MD, Hal Straus. Daniel Lieberman. Jessie Inchauspe. Lisa Mosconi, PhD. Jenara Nerenberg. Rachel Clarke, MD. Paul Kalanithi, MD. Daniel J. Siegel, MD. Daniel Lieberman, MD, Michael E. Long. Dr. Jenna Macciochi. Emily Oster, PhD. Daniel G. Amen, MD.

chapters.title: Your ability to free yourself from dead weight
chapters.text: Section placeholder. This entry groups contributor names under a Sports and Fitness tag. The names below appear in the original source for reference. Tom Nichols, PhD. Patrick Radden Keefe. Elizabeth L. Cline. Carl T. Bergstrom, Jevin D. West. Jenara Nerenberg. Christian Davenport.

chapters.title: Leave your comfort zone to unlock your true potential
chapters.text:
Dawn breaks with an adventure that changes everything. At 5 am, the billionaire picks up his guests in a helicopter for a "field trip." He later announces that they are heading back to the airport, and their next destination is Agra, India.

During their memorable visit to the Taj Mahal, he shared another factor that separates successes from failures. He had come to understand that successful people chose to do the things the average person would find difficult. They kept repeating those hard tasks until they became habits.

Your brain keeps growing if you challenge yourself to expand your horizons and leave your comfort zone.

Still, the mogul pointed out that this method calls for a steady process with specific phases and no shortcuts. His approach demands constant attention, and adopters have to walk through each phase, since there are no shortcuts. Your willpower can adapt to whatever limits you set. By widening your worldview, you develop your brain even further.

It is vital to step outside your comfort zone to unlock your true potential and build the stamina that success demands. In the end, your comfort zone keeps you on the same ground you are used to. So change is necessary if you want to succeed.

Many people give up halfway through their self discovery journey. The successful ones first had to shape their emotions, bodies, and spirits to match their new winning mindset. Without that flexibility, those people would not have come close to the success they reached.

chapters.title: It is not enough to wake up early
chapters.text: Section placeholder. This entry gathers contributor names from related Headway summaries. The names below appear in the original source for reference. Tom Nichols, PhD. Patrick Radden Keefe. Elizabeth L. Cline. Carl T. Bergstrom, Jevin D. West. Jenara Nerenberg. Christian Davenport.

chapters.title: Sleep deprivation and oversleeping will erode your productivity
chapters.text:
While in the Roman catacombs, the underground tunnels where the ancient Romans buried their dead, the billionaire explained that his next lesson would touch on the essentials of sleep. It felt fitting to hold that session in a place where the occupants were in a permanent deep sleep.

This session explored the downsides of technologies that steal our sleep time. How you spend the latter part of the day matters as much as how you spend the early part. Staying up late makes it hard to wake at 5 am and put the 20/20/20 formula to work. Over time, this wears down your productivity, performance, and health.

Lack of sleep cuts productivity, drags down performance, lowers general happiness, and harms health.

Just as sleep deprivation shrinks performance and life span, researchers have found that oversleeping is harmful to well being. Balance is the key. The Spellbinder said that seven hours of sleep is ideal. Once you get good sleep and run your morning routines, you should focus on specific tasks for the first hour and a half of work.

When you do this, fight the urge to give in to distractions, and then find time to rest between long stretches of work. Pushing yourself too hard only drains energy and lowers productivity. Good rest keeps the creative juices flowing. You can also use that time to learn new things that broaden your horizons.

Melatonin is the chemical that tells your body it is time to sleep. When you sleep, and here quality matters as much as quantity, your neurons shrink by about 60 percent as cerebrospinal fluid washes through your brain.
